summary: Martin Fowler explains Domain Specific Languages (DSLs) for a non-technical audience, defining them as limited-expressiveness programming languages focused on particular domains. He traces their history from Unix and Lisp communities through to their modern resurgence via Ruby and Rails. The piece argues that DSLs primarily benefit developer productivity through improved code comprehension, with the more ambitious goal of making code readable (though not necessarily writable) by business people. Fowler cautions against the dream of non-programmers writing software, instead emphasizing DSLs as collaboration tools between business and technical teams.
key_points: DSLs are not new—they've existed since early Unix and include familiar tools like SQL, CSS, and regular expressions
Internal DSLs are idiomatic code in a host language (fluent interfaces), while external DSLs are separate languages that get parsed
The primary benefit is improved code comprehension and maintainability; the more ambitious benefit is business readability
Business-readable DSLs yield most benefits at far less cost than business-writable ones
DSLs don't add significant complexity beyond existing frameworks—they're essentially fancy facades that can make frameworks easier to use
money_quotes: A Domain Specific Language (DSL) is a computer programming language of limited expressiveness focused on a particular domain.
You get most of the benefit of business facing DSLs by doing enough to allow business people to be able to read the rules.
Getting DSLs to be business readable is far less effort than business writable, but yields most of the benefits.
That was the intent of COBOL, and I don't think there's any reason to think that DSLs will succeed where COBOL (and so many others failed).
What I think is important is that DSLs allow business people and developers to collaborate more effectively because they can talk about a common set of precise rules that are the executable code.
In essence a DSL is nothing more than a fancy facade over a framework.
key_insight: DSLs are most valuable not as tools for non-programmers to write code, but as a shared language that enables business people and developers to collaborate around precise, executable rules.
